title: V.Narasimha Reddy vs P.Ravindra Reddy on 30 November, 2023
body: V.Narasimha Reddy vs P.Ravindra Reddy on 30 November, 2023 THE HON'BLE SRI JUSTICE T.MALLIKARJUNA RAO APPEAL SUIT NO.967 OF 2012 JUDGMENT: 1. The Appeal, under Section 96 of the Code of the Civil Procedure, 1908, is filed by the appellant/plaintiff challenging the decree and Judgment dated 04.06.2012 in O.S.No.3 of 2009 passed by the learned Principal District Judge, Kurnool (for short, 'the trial Court'). The Appellant is the Plaintiff, who filed the suit in O.S.No.3 of 2009 seeking recovery of Rs.11,30,000/- towards principal and Rs.8,13,600/- towards interest, totalling Rs.19,43,600/- from the Defendant with interest at the rate of 24% per annum. The Respondent is the Defendant in the said suit. 2. The parties will hereinafter be referred to as arrayed before the trial Court. 3. The facts leading to the present Appeal, in a nutshell, are as under: Defendant is indebted to Plaintiff and executed a promissory note on 01.01.2006 for Rs.11,30,000/-, agreeing to repay the same on demand with interest @ 24% per annum. Still, Defendant, despite several demands made by Plaintiff, did not repay the suit promissory note. Ultimately, the Plaintiff issued a legal notice dated 22.12.2008, but the Defendant did not make any payment. The Defendant is 2 T.M.R., J A.S. No.967 of 2012 neither an agriculturist nor a small farmer. Hence, he is not entitled to the Agricultural Debt Relief Act benefits. 4. (a) In the written statement, Defendant denied the claims made in the plaint. The Defendant asserted that on 01.12.2000, he borrowed Rs.1,30,000/- from Plaintiff, repaid it within six months, and executed the promissory note in question. The Defendant emphasized the close familial relationship, as the Plaintiff's wife was the Defendant's sister. Due to this closeness, the Defendant didn't press for the return of the promissory note. The Defendant alleged that the Plaintiff altered the note, changing the amount and date before filing the suit. Additionally, the witnesses listed in the suit promissory note were not present during the original execution but signed later as friends of the Plaintiff. (b) The Defendant asserts that significant disputes arose between the parties after the death of the Plaintiff's wife, particularly concerning rival claims over the factory premises. Subsequently, several cases were filed post-2003. The Plaintiff filed O.S.No.27 of 2003 and O.S.No.14 of 2005 against the Defendant, claiming amounts based on promissory notes. The Defendant contested these suits, asserting that the notes were executed to recognize Plaintiff's share in a Board Factory (S.V.Foods) run by Defendant. Both suits were dismissed on 04.04.2006. The Defendant highlighted the enmity between them since filing these suits, making it implausible for 3 T.M.R., J A.S. No.967 of 2012 Defendant to borrow under the alleged promissory note. The Defendant questioned Plaintiff's capacity to lend Rs.11,30,000/-, disputed the authenticity of the signature on the postal acknowledgement, and claimed non-receipt of the alleged demand notice, suggesting that Plaintiff manipulated the acknowledgement with Defendant's address. 5. Based on the above pleadings, the trial Court framed the following issues: (1) Whether the suit promissory note dated 01.01.2006 is true, valid and binding on the Defendant? (2) Whether Defendant paid an amount of Rs.1,30,000/- to Plaintiff in respect of the promissory note dated 01.01.2006? (3) Whether the Defendant discharged the amount covered under the promissory note dated 01.01.2006? (4) Whether there is any material alteration in the promissory note dated 01.01.2006? (5) Whether the Plaintiff is entitled to recover an amount of Rs.11,30,000/- from the Defendant with future interest at 24% per annum from the date of suit till the date of realization? (6) To what relief? The trial court, subsequently, as per the orders dated 20.09.2011 passed in I.A. No.1518 of 2011, issues No.2 and 3 were re-casted as follows: (2) Whether the defendant paid Rs.1,30,000/- to the plaintiff in respect of promissory note dated 01.01.2000? (3) Whether the Defendant discharged the amount covered under the promissory note dated 01.01.2000? 4 T.M.R., J A.S. No.967 of 2012 6. During the trial, on behalf of the Plaintiff, P.Ws.1 and 2 were examined, and Exs.A.1 to A.13 were marked. On behalf of the Defendant, D.W.1 was examined, and Exs.B.1 to B.4 were marked. 7. After completing the trial and hearing the arguments of both sides, the trial Court dismissed the suit. Aggrieved the same, the appellant/plaintiff preferred the present Appeal. 8. Sri J.U.M.V.Prasad, the learned counsel representing the Appellant/Plaintiff, argued that Defendant's acknowledgement of the promissory notes' execution is undisputed. He contended that the trial Court erred by deeming the Defendant's contentions unchallenged merely because the Plaintiff did not file a rejoinder. This, he argued, is illegal and contrary to established legal principles. The counsel criticized the trial Court for overlooking the Defendant's claim of material alterations in the promissory note without seeking expert examination. He asserted that the trial Court should have accepted the Plaintiff's version, supported by strong evidence, and granted the decree. Learned counsel further contended that the trial Court's findings lacked a legal basis, failed to provide sufficient reasons for disregarding presented evidence, and, consequently, resulted in misdirection, leading to the dismissal of the suit based on assumptions and presumptions rather than a fair evaluation of the substantial evidence. 5 T.M.R., J A.S. No.967 of 2012 9. Per contra, Sri K. Satyanarayana Murthy, learned counsel representing the Respondent/Defendant, contends that the trial Court correctly appreciated the case facts and reached a correct conclusion. The reasons given by the trial Court do not require any interference. 10. With regard to the pleadings in the suit and the findings recorded by the Trial Court, the following points would arise for determination: 1) Was the trial Court justified in holding that the Plaintiff failed to prove the suit promissory note is true, valid and binding on the Defendant? 2) Does the Judgment passed by the trial Court need any interference? POINT NO.1: 11. During the trial, Plaintiff, examined as PW.1, was supported by D. Gajendra Reddy, also examined as PW.2, the 2nd attestor under Ex.A.1 promissory note. Both testified that Defendant executed Ex.A.1 on 01.01.2006 for Rs.11,30,000/-, agreeing to repay on demand with 24% interest, which Defendant failed to fulfill. A legal notice (Ex.A.2) was issued before filing the suit, and the Defendant acknowledged its receipt (Ex.A.3). 12. In his defence, the Defendant examined as DW.1, denied executing the Ex.A.1 promissory note, asserting that he borrowed Rs.1,30,000/- in 2000. He claimed the date and amount were altered 6 T.M.R., J A.S. No.967 of 2012 by the Plaintiff. Despite this, Defendant did not seek expert opinion on Ex.A.1 but relied on circumstantial evidence. It is not disputed that the Plaintiff married the Defendant's elder sister in 1980. In the cross- examination, DW.1 testified that both families lived together until 2003 from 1992, and DW.1 admitted the Plaintiff's role as a sleeping partner in the business by the name S.V. Foods from 1994 to 2003. DW.1 refuted the borrowing amount covered under Ex.A.1 promissory note. 13. In a decision G. Vasu V. Syed Yaseen Sifuddin1 , the composite High Court of Andhra Pradesh held that: "Once the defendant shows either by direct evidence or circumstantial evidence or by use of other presumptions of law or fact that the promissory note is not supported by consideration in the manner stated in the promissory note or the manner stated in the suit notice or the pleading, the evidential burden shifts to the plaintiff and the legal burden of the plaintiff is revived, i.e., to prove that the promissory note is supported by consideration and at that stage. The presumption of law covered by S.118 " disappears and no longer exists". "It is further held that once both parties have adduced evidence the Court has to consider the same and the burden of proof loses all its importance". 14. In Bharat Barrel and Drum Manufacturing Company, V. Amin Chand Payrelal2 , the Hon'ble Apex Court was held thus : "Once execution of the promissory note is admitted, the presumption under Section 118(a) would arise that it is supported by consideration. Such a presumption is rebuttable. The defendant can prove the non-existence of consideration by raising 1 AIR 1987 AP 139 2 (1999) 3 SCC 35 7 T.M.R., J A.S. No.967 of 2012 a probable defence. If the defendant is proved to have discharged the initial onus of proof showing that the existence of consideration was improbable or doubtful or the same was illegal, the onus would shift to the plaintiff who will be obliged to prove it as a matter of fact and upon its failure to prove would disentitle him to the grant of relief on the basis of the negotiable instrument. The burden upon the defendant of proving the non- existence of the consideration can be either direct or by bringing on record the preponderance of probabilities by reference to the circumstances upon which he relies. In such an event the plaintiff is entitled under law to rely upon all the evidence led in the case including that of the plaintiff as well. In case, where the defendant fails to discharge the initial onus of proof by showing the non-existence of the consideration, the plaintiff would invariably be held entitled to the benefit of presumption arising under Section 118(a) in his favour. The Court may not insist upon the defendant to disprove the existence of consideration by leading direct evidence as existence of negative evidence is neither possible nor contemplated and even if led is to be seen with a doubt. The bare denial of the passing of the consideration apparently does not appear to be any defence. Something which is probable has to be brought on record for getting the benefit of shifting the onus of proving to the plaintiff. To disprove the presumption the defendant has to bring on record such facts and circumstances, upon consideration of which the Court may either believe that the consideration did not exist or its non-existence was so probable that a prudent man would, under the circumstances of the case, shall act upon the plea that it did not exist." 15. In M.S. Narayana Menon @ Mani v. State of Kerala and another3, the Supreme Court noted the presumption under Sections 118(a) and 139 of the Negotiable Instruments Act to be rebuttable in nature, and the standard of proof evidently is preponderance of probabilities. Inference of preponderance of probabilities can be drawn from the materials on record and by references to the circumstances upon which the accused relies. The accused was not necessarily 3 2006 6 SCC 39 8 T.M.R., J A.S. No.967 of 2012 required to disprove the prosecution case, and whether, in the given facts and circumstances, the initial burden that had been discharged by an accused would be a question of fact and a matter relating to the appreciation of evidence. 16. The Defendant asserts that disputes between the Plaintiff and himself intensified after the death of Plaintiff's wife, leading to a series of legal battles starting in 2003. The Defendant points to the existence of a fierce rivalry between them. To support this claim, the Defendant presented evidence in the form of suits filed by the Plaintiff. Notably, Exs.B.1 to B.3 were marked during PW.1's cross-examination based on his admission. Furthermore, Ex.B.4 was introduced during D.W.1's chief examination. Exhibit B.1 is a certified copy of the chief affidavit filed by the present Plaintiff as PW.1 in O.S.No.27 of 2003 before the IV Additional District Judge, Kurnool. Ex.B.2 contains the admission made by the Plaintiff during his cross-examination in connection with Ex.B.1. The said admission reads as follows: "Except Exs.A.1 and A.2, there are no other promissory notes executed by the Defendant in my favour". 17. The record indicates that the deposition in Ex.B.1 recorded on 21.11.2005. However, the alleged transaction for the present suit is stated to have occurred on 01.01.2006. Therefore, in line with the trial court's observation and considering the timing of PW.1's cross- 9 T.M.R., J A.S. No.967 of 2012 examination in O.S.No.27 of 2003, the transaction related to Ex.A.1 need not be included in that deposition. 18. On the other hand, the Ex.B.3 document was also marked in the PW.1's cross-examination. PW.1 has not disputed the contents of the Ex.B.3 document. In the cross-examination of PW.1, he admitted as follows: "Ex.B.3 is the list of debts payable by me and debts payable to me. Ex.B.3 contains the debts payable by the Defendant. I have also mentioned in Ex.B.3 the list of pronotes that are executed by the Defendant in my favour. Ex.B.3 is scribed by me on my own handwriting. In Ex.B.3, I have mentioned that Ravi executed pronote dated 01.01.2000 for Rs.1,30,000/- and another pronote dated 01.01.2001 for Rs.5,00,000/- and another pronote dated 01.12.2000 for Rs.6,00,000/-, Ravi mentioned in Ex.B.3 is refers the Defendant". 19. It is settled law that the best evidence in the case is the admission of the opposite party. Admissions are valuable evidence because when a party himself admits to being true, it may be reasonably presumed to be so until the presumption is rebutted. The fact admitted must be taken to be established. The effect of admission is merely to shift the onus of disproving on the party, making them unless a plea of estoppels can be successfully invoked. It is well settled that admission is the best evidence that an opposing party can rely upon, though not conclusive, is a decisive matter unless 10 T.M.R., J A.S. No.967 of 2012 successfully withdrawn or proved erroneous. There cannot be better evidence than one's admission. 20. As such, the controversy will have to be decided based on oral evidence of PW.1 and DW.1. It would be a travesty of truth, justice and reasonableness to throw overboard the entire evidence and reject the claim lock, stock and barrel for the only reason that still better evidence has not been placed before the Court. Absolute certainty is not the requirement under Sec.3 of the Evidence Act. In a civil case, rival contentions and rival evidence will have to be considered, assessed, evaluated and weighed to conclude whether the burden on the Plaintiff has been discharged. 21. If prudence is the real test, it prescribes differing standards of proof in varying circumstances. Its requirements preclude any Procrustean bed of uniformly rigid rules for each type of case. 22. As aptly noted by the trial court, Ex.B.3, along with PW.1's admission, establishes the existence of a prior promissory note dated 01.01.2000, amounting to Rs.1,30,000/-, executed by the Defendant in favour of the Plaintiff. According to the Defendant, he cleared this debt within six months but, due to their close relationship, left the promissory note with Plaintiff. The Plaintiff allegedly made material alterations to both the date and the amount, leading to the present suit. The trial court, after careful consideration of the evidence, noted 11 T.M.R., J A.S. No.967 of 2012 the common knowledge that altering the date from "01.01.2000" to "01.01.2006" and the amount from "Rs.1,30,000/-" to "Rs.11,30,000/-" is easily achievable, especially since these details were not mentioned in words in Ex.A.1. In light of the Defendant's plea regarding material alteration, the trial court expected the Plaintiff to respond with the following pleas: (i) The earlier suit promissory note date 01.01.2000 for Rs.1,30,000/- executed by Defendant was true. Still, he did not discharge the said debt, and the present suit promissory note is different from the earlier promissory note. (ii) That the earlier promissory note dated 01.01.2000 executed by Defendant for Rs.1,30,000/- was true and his plea of discharge was also true, but Plaintiff had returned that promissory note to Defendant, and the same is not materially altered to file the present suit, as alleged. The present suit promissory note is a different one. 23. In M.Narsinga Rao V. State of Andhra Pradesh4 , the Hon'ble Supreme Court held that: 15. The word "proof" needs to be understood in the sense it is defined in the Evidence Act because proof depends upon the admissibility of evidence. A fact is said to be proved when, after considering the matters before it, the Court either believes it to exist or considers its existence so probable that a prudent man ought, under the circumstances of the particular case, to act upon the supposition that it exists. This is the definition given for the word "proved" in the Evidence Act . What is required is the production of such materials on which the Court can reasonably act to reach the supposition that a fact exists. Proof of the fact depends upon the degree of probability of its having existed. The standard required for reaching the supposition is that of a prudent man acting in any important matter concerning him. Fletcher Moulton L.J. in Hawkins v. Powells Tillery Steam Coal Company, Ltd., observed this: 4 (2001) 1 SCC 691 12 T.M.R., J A.S. No.967 of 2012 "Proof does not mean proof to rigid mathematical demonstration, because that is impossible; it must mean such evidence as would induce a reasonable man to come to a particular conclusion." 16. The said observation has stood the test of time and can now be followed as the standard of proof. In concluding, the Court can use the process of inferences to be drawn from facts produced or proved. Such inferences are akin to presumptions in law. The law gives absolute discretion to the Court to presume the existence of any fact that it thinks likely to have happened. In that process, the Court may have regard to the common course of natural events, human conduct, and public or private business vis-a-vis the facts of the particular case. The discretion is envisaged in Section 114 of the Evidence Act. 24. The Appellant's counsel contends that the trial Court ought not to visualize the Plaintiff's pleas in the suit. This Court views that the said submission is incorrect. When the Defendant has taken a specific plea that there was a promissory note transaction on 01.01.2000 and borrowed Rs.1,30,000/- from Plaintiff, and the Ex.A.1 suit transaction is materially altered one, Plaintiff is expected to explain his stand on the said contention. 25. The effect of material alteration has been provided under Section 87 of the Negotiable Instrument Act, which is as follows:- 87. Effect of material alteration - Any material alteration of a negotiable instrument renders the same void as against anyone who is a party to it at the time of making such alteration and does not consent to it unless it was made to carry out the common intention of the original parties; Alteration by indorsee - And any such alteration, if made by an indorsee, discharges his indorser from all liability to him in respect of the consideration thereof. The provisions of this section are subject to those of sections 20 , 49 , 86 and 125 . " 13 T.M.R., J A.S. No.967 of 2012 26. In respect to material alteration in the case of Loon Karan Sethia, etc. vs. Ivan E. John and others. Etc.,5 the Hon'ble Apex Court has held that: A material alteration is on which varies the rights, liabilities, or legal position of the parties as ascertained by the deed in its original state, or otherwise varies the legal effect of the instrument as expressed originally, or reduces to certainty some provision which was originally unascertained and such void, or which may otherwise prejudice the party bound by the deed as originally executed. 27. The principle underlying material alteration is well-settled. It pre-supposes that there was a writing to which the executant of the negotiable instrument was a party and that writing was given the go- by by a conscious covert or overt act on the part of the creditor, resulting in a material alteration of the instrument. By establishing that a negotiable instrument has been altered in a superfluous sense will not suffice to prevent the creditor from instituting an action on such an altered instrument. The debtor complaining of a material alteration should not rest content by establishing that there was a formal alteration which is innocuous and superfluous, but he should also further establish that there was a material alteration in the sense that what is sought to be put in Court was not the contract between the parties and was not what was intended between the parties at or about the time when it was executed. 5 1977 AIR 336 14 T.M.R., J A.S. No.967 of 2012 28. The trial Court has observed that Plaintiff has not disputed the earlier promissory note transaction dated 01.01.2000 for Rs.1,30,000/-. It is not the Plaintiff's stand that Defendant did not discharge the said debt. It is not the Plaintiff's case that after the discharge of the debt of Rs.1,30,000/- (vide original of Ex.B.4 transaction), he returned the said promissory note. Plaintiff had not taken any of the pleas by filing a rejoinder to clarify his stand; not only that, in the chief examination, he has not categorically clarified his stand on the original of Ex.B.4 transaction, except mentioning that the Defendant's plea is false. However, non-filing of rejoinder by Plaintiff does not amount to an admission of pleadings made in a written statement. Plaintiff can file a rejoinder bringing to notice of the Court certain subsequent events without raising pleas inconsistent with the original pleading in the plaint and without altering the cause of action stated in the plaint. At this stage, it is pertinent to note that, as already observed, PW.1 admitted in cross-examination an earlier promissory note (copy of Ex.B.4, dated 01.01.2000). Given the same, the trial Court is correct in holding that an inference can be drawn that the earlier promissory note executed by Defendant in Plaintiff's favour on 01.01.2000 for Rs.1,30,000/- is very much available with Plaintiff. 29. In Civil cases, the preponderance of probability constitutes a sufficient ground for decision if the facts and circumstances are such 15 T.M.R., J A.S. No.967 of 2012 that no reasonable man would draw a particular inference from them or if the degree of probability in the case is such that as to include any hypothesis besides the one to be proved then the party who relies on a particular theory cannot be said to have discharged the onus of proof of establishing that theory. But, if the evidence strongly preponders in favour of any of the two theories set up, the Court is entitled to act on it. 30. In K.Mariappan V. M.P. Kumarasamy6 , the High Court of Madras held that: 21. The standard of proof evidentially is principles of preponderance of probability. Inference of preponderance of probability can be drawn not only from the materials on record but also by reference to the circumstances upon which he relies. 31. In R.Puthunainar Alhithan V. P.H.Pandian7 , the Hon'ble Supreme Court held that an inference from the proved facts must be so probable that if the Court believes, from the proved facts, that the facts do exist, it must be held that the fact has been proved. The inference of proof of that fact could be drawn from the given objective, direct or circumstantial. 32. It is also not in dispute that the Plaintiff filed the suit in O.S.No.27 of 2003 based on the promissory note dated 01.12.2000 for Rs.6,00,000/- and also filed another suit in O.S.No.14 of 2005 based on the promissory note dated 01.01.2001 for Rs.5,00,000/-; the 6 2021 0 Supreme(Mad) 2367 7 (1996) 3 SCC 624 16 T.M.R., J A.S. No.967 of 2012 Defendant contested those two suits and ultimately, those two suits were dismissed on 04.04.2006; the Plaintiff preferred the Appeals before this Court and the Appeals are pending. The filing of the suits in O.S.No.27 of 2003 and O.S.No.14 of 2005 clearly shows that since 2003, the relationship between Plaintiff and Defendant has been strained, and they have been fighting in Court. Thus, it is established that even by the date of alleged Ex.A.1 promissory note transaction, i.e., on 01.01.2006, they were inimical with each other and as already observed by the trial Court, if that were so, it will be highly improbable and illogical to accept that the Plaintiff would lend such a huge amount of Rs.11,30,000/- to the Defendant on 01.01.2006 and this is bereft of commonsense also. The trial court rightly pointed out that there was no answer from the Plaintiff as to what made him advance the loan to the Defendant when they were fighting with each other in Court. So, from these circumstances, the trial Court observed that the only inference that can be drawn is that Plaintiff must have materially altered the date and the amount of the earlier promissory note available to him and filed the suit. The trial Court rightly questioned the logic behind such a transaction amid ongoing legal disputes. 33. The trial court has considered that the Plaintiff has been an income tax assessee since 1975. Still, he did not produce the income tax returns before the Court to show that the said transaction had not been shown in the income tax returns. The trial Court, noting the 17 T.M.R., J A.S. No.967 of 2012 Plaintiff's reluctance, infers that the document might not be in the income tax returns, making the Plaintiff accountable to the tax department. The Court also dismisses PW.2's evidence, highlighting his distant residence from Kurnool and lack of explanation for his presence during the alleged Ex.A.1 transaction. The reliance on documents Exs.A.1 to A.4 to demonstrate financial capacity is deemed irrelevant to establish the genuineness of Ex.A.1, as concluded by the trial Court through a comparative analysis of the evidence presented by both parties. 34. The Defendant successfully fulfilled the initial evidential burden by presenting plausible evidence that cast doubt on the authenticity of the promissory note and the passing of consideration. Consequently, the presumption under section 118 of the N.I Act ceased to have any effect and was no longer relevant. Trite, the proposition of law is that witnesses might lie, but the circumstances would not do so. 35. The Defendant was successful in showing the improbability of the execution of the promissory note and passing of consideration. Therefore, the evidence with respect to the execution of the pronote coupled with the said circumstances creates doubt and shows a probability that the present case has been foisted against the Defendant for the ulterior motive by PWs.1 and 2. The evidence adduced by the Plaintiff regarding the payment of consideration at the time of the execution of Ex.A.1 promissory note cannot be accepted. 18 T.M.R., J A.S. No.967 of 2012 36. As a result, the presumption under section 118 of N.I Act disappears and becomes functus officio and the evidential burden shifts to the Plaintiff, who also has the legal burden arising out of the pleadings to prove the consideration. After careful consideration of the entire evidence, I am of the view that the Plaintiff has not discharged the legal burden, as such, he cannot once again rely on the presumption of sec.118 of N.I Act. Therefore, it can be said that the findings arrived at by the trial Court are perverse. 37. After careful consideration, the trial Court correctly appreciated the evidence. There is no reason for this Court to arrive at a different conclusion than the one arrived at by the trial Court. I am of the view that the findings arrived at by the trial Court are correct, and no justifiable reasons have been shown by the Appellant/Plaintiff for arriving at different conclusions. I agree with the conclusion reached by the trial Court. 38. Accordingly, the Points are answered in favour of the Defendant. Given the preceding discussion, the view taken by the trial court does not call for any interference, and this Appeal fails and is hereby dismissed. The impugned Decree and Judgment passed by the trial court is upheld. 19 T.M.R., J A.S. No.967 of 2012 39. As a result, the Appeal is hereby dismissed with costs by confirming the Decree and Judgment in O.S. No.3 of 2009, dated 04.06.2012, passed by the learned Principal District Judge, Kurnool. Miscellaneous petitions pending, if any, in this Appeal, shall stand closed. ________________________________ JUSTICE T. MALLIKARJUNA RAO Date: 30.11.2023 SAK 20 T.M.R., J A.S. No.967 of 2012 THE HON'BLE SRI JUSTICE T.MALLIKARJUNA RAO APPEAL SUIT NO.967 OF 2012 Date: 30.11.2023 SAK
